schools.summary: Gill: the body of laws was finished in the preceding chapter; here are added rules concerning vows, which a man was not obliged to make but did of his own freewill and good pleasure.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
the law of vows
the freewill offering of the heart

schools.summary: Gill: this concerned the children of Israel only, the Gentiles being excluded.
Keil & Delitzsch: 'make a special vow' — souls were to be to the LORD according to Moses' valuation, the person vowed redeemed by the fixed estimation.

themes: When a man shall make a singular vow
the persons shall be for the LORD by thy estimation
devotion expressed in what could be rendered

schools.summary: Gill: the estimation was not left to the man's or priest's pleasure but fixed by these rules, in proportion to the age.
Keil & Delitzsch: fifty shekels of silver for a male from twenty to sixty years old, after the shekel of the sanctuary.

themes: of the male from twenty... unto sixty years old... fifty shekels
after the shekel of the sanctuary
the vow guarded by a fixed, just scale

schools.summary: Gill: the female of the same full age, twenty to sixty, valued at thirty shekels.

Clarke: the woman valued at thirty shekels, a little more than half the man's, for the reason that she could not be of so much use in the service of the sanctuary, and was therefore of less working value.
Ellicott: as the woman is the weaker vessel and her labour of less value, her estimation is lower.

themes: if it be a female... thirty shekels
a measure of labour, not personal worth
God values the soul without respect of persons

schools.summary: Gill: not that a child of five vows, but a grown person says 'the estimation of this little one be upon me,' and pays the value.
Clarke: the boy vowed redeemed for twenty shekels, the girl for ten, just one half, on the same principle of value.

themes: from five... unto twenty years old... twenty shekels... ten shekels
the scale of service-value
the young as truly dedicated as the grown

schools.summary: Gill: one under a month old might be vowed but not estimated, no valuation being made for it.
Clarke: the male child five shekels, the female three, nearly equal being both in infancy; none vowed under a month, the firstborn being already the LORD's property.

themes: from a month old... five shekels... three shekels
none under a month estimated
the firstborn already the LORD's

schools.summary: Gill: this is of one almost past his labour, when it is high time to leave off business.
Clarke: the old man and woman, being nearly past labour, were nearly of equal value, fifteen and ten shekels, on the same ratio as the children and for the same reason.
Ellicott: being almost past labour, the old man is next in value to the child.

themes: from sixty years old and above... fifteen shekels... ten shekels
the scale closing low as it began
the gift of a life nearly run still welcome

schools.summary: Gill: if he is so poor that he is not able to pay the value set upon him, the case is provided for.
Ellicott: the man pleading poverty was to appear before the priest, who examined his circumstances and taxed him accordingly, with a minimum during the second temple of one shekel.

themes: if he be poorer than thy estimation... the priest shall value him
according to his ability
the willing heart accepted by what it has (2 Cor. 8:12)

schools.summary: Gill: this is of creatures used in sacrifice — bullocks, sheep, goats, rams, and lambs — devoted to the Lord.
Keil & Delitzsch: everything given of such to Jehovah was to be holy and not exchanged, a good animal for a bad or a bad for a good.

themes: if it be a beast, whereof men bring an offering unto the LORD
all that any man giveth... shall be holy
the gift given to God for good

schools.summary: Gill, with Abarbinel: 'alter' is for one of another kind (herd for flock), 'change' for one of the same kind; neither is allowed.
Keil & Delitzsch: he shall not change a good for a bad or a bad for a good; if he change beast for beast, then it and the exchange shall both be holy.

themes: He shall not alter it nor change it
both it and the exchange... shall be holy
craft with God only doubling the obligation

schools.summary: Gill: this is of creatures not offered besides the clean kinds, or of blemished beasts unfit for the altar.
Ellicott: an unclean beast not belonging to the three sacrificial kinds, which cannot be offered, is presented to the priest.

themes: if it be any unclean beast... he shall present the beast before the priest
the unfit valued in money
nothing devoted in sincerity wasted

schools.summary: Gill: the priest put a price upon it according to its worth as it appeared to him.
Keil & Delitzsch: the priest valued it good or bad, and if the owner would redeem it, he added a fifth above the valuation (v.13).

themes: the priest shall value it, whether it be good or bad
so shall it be
the priest's appraisal a guard of order and honesty

schools.summary: Gill: this is for the owner determined to have his vowed beast again at any rate; he adds a fifth part to the estimation.
Clarke: the added fifth was probably intended to prevent rash vows and covetous redemptions; the priest alone valued the thing, and a fifth was added by him who wished to redeem it.

themes: if he will at all redeem it... add a fifth part
a check on rash vows
devotion not a thing of second thoughts

schools.summary: Gill: the house was set apart for sacred service, devoted to holy uses, that it might be sold and the money laid out on sacrifices or the repairs of the temple; other goods are comprehended under this, with Jewish rules for debts and a wife's dowry.

themes: when a man shall sanctify his house to be holy unto the LORD
as the priest shall estimate it, so shall it stand
the whole of life open to be given

schools.summary: Gill: a house set apart might be redeemed, by another paying the price or by the original owner adding a fifth part; citing Maimonides, the house, whether in a walled city or a village, might always be redeemed.

themes: if he that sanctified it will redeem his house... add the fifth part
always redeemable
the charge honouring the original devotion

schools.summary: Gill: the field of possession was the inheritance from his father, distinct from a field of purchase; only part might be devoted, that the family keep a livelihood and the estate not pass from the tribe.
Keil & Delitzsch: if any one sanctified of the field of his possession, his hereditary property, the valuation was made according to the seed it required.

themes: sanctify... some part of a field of his possession
thy estimation... according to the seed thereof
the family inheritance guarded even in devotion

schools.summary: Gill: this is of one who sanctifies the field at or just after the jubilee year, so that a purchaser comes at once and the full estimation stands.
Keil & Delitzsch: if he sanctified his field immediately after the jubilee, it was to stand according to the valuation, with no alteration.

themes: If he sanctify his field from the year of jubile
according to thy estimation it shall stand
the price tied to the years of use

schools.summary: Gill: this is of one who sanctifies his field some years after the jubilee, halfway or nearer to the next.
Keil & Delitzsch: the priest reckoned the money according to the years remaining to the jubilee, and it was abated from the valuation.

themes: if he sanctify his field after the jubile... according to the years that remain
abated from thy estimation
valued by what it yields before the return

schools.summary: Gill: this is of one desirous and determined to have his field again, repenting that he parted with it.
Ellicott: the low value per acre was designed that the field should be redeemed by him; the rule included his wife and heirs, any of whom might redeem it.

themes: if he... will in any wise redeem it... add the fifth part
it shall be assured to him
the law leaning to keep the inheritance in the family

schools.summary: Gill: this is of one who will not give the fifth besides, but lets the field be disposed of for the holy uses he devoted it to.
Ellicott: of one base enough to forego the privilege of redemption, showing no desire to perpetuate his family name, or who further sells the field to another.

themes: if he will not redeem the field, or... have sold the field
it shall not be redeemed any more
the loss falling on his own neglect

schools.summary: Gill: the field then went out of the hand of him that bought it.
Keil & Delitzsch: the field, going out in the jubilee, became holy to the LORD as a devoted field, the possession of the priest.

themes: the field, when it goeth out in the jubile, shall be holy unto the LORD
the possession thereof shall be the priest's
the neglected vow made good in the end

schools.summary: Gill: this is a field bought with his own money from one in distress, which by the former law returned to the original owner at the jubilee.
Ellicott: a field acquired by purchase, his only till the next jubilee, when it reverts to its original owner (cf. Lev. 25:25-28).

themes: if a man sanctify... a field which he hath bought
which is not of the fields of his possession
devotion not reaching beyond his right

schools.summary: Gill: the priest estimated the field and set a price according to the years remaining to the jubilee.
Keil & Delitzsch: he gave the estimation that day as a holy thing unto the LORD, reckoned to the year of jubilee.

themes: the priest shall reckon... the worth... even unto the year of the jubile
as a holy thing unto the LORD
giving God the worth of what was truly his

schools.summary: Gill: it returned not to him that sanctified it, nor to him that bought it of the temple treasurer, but to the original proprietor of whom it was first bought.
Ellicott: in accordance with Lev. 25:23-28, the field returned at the jubilee to the hereditary owner, of whom the vower had bought it.

themes: In the year of the jubile the field shall return unto him of whom it was bought
to him to whom the possession... did belong
the LORD's land kept in its family

schools.summary: Gill: the sanctuary shekel was the standard of all shekels, of full weight and worth, and the payment made in such full weight.
Clarke: a standard shekel, kept in the sanctuary to try and regulate all the weights in the land.

themes: all thy estimation... according to the shekel of the sanctuary
twenty gerahs shall be the shekel
the LORD's standard the rule of all

schools.summary: Gill: the firstlings are excepted from being sanctified, for the reason that they are already the LORD's.
Keil & Delitzsch: what belonged to the LORD by law could not be dedicated to him by a vow, especially the firstborn of clean cattle (cf. Exod. 13:1-2).

themes: the firstling of the beasts... no man shall sanctify it
it is the LORD's
the first and best already God's by prior claim

schools.summary: Gill: distinguishes this from the firstling of unclean creatures redeemed with a lamb, reading it of such as were vowed for the reparation of the sanctuary.
Keil & Delitzsch: the unclean beast was redeemed according to the valuation with a fifth added, or if not redeemed, sold according to the valuation.

themes: if it be of an unclean beast... redeem it... add a fifth part
or... it shall be sold
even the unfit firstborn brought into the LORD's account

schools.summary: Gill: distinguishes 'devoting' from 'sanctifying' — the devoted thing carried a curse or execration upon misuse, and so was absolute and irrevocable, unalienable, not to be sold or redeemed, whereas sanctified things might be.
Clarke: no devoted thing might be sold or redeemed; every devoted thing is most holy unto the LORD.

themes: no devoted thing... shall be sold or redeemed
every devoted thing is most holy unto the LORD
the gift from which there is no taking back

schools.summary: Gill: reads this not of men devoted to the LORD (v.28) but of such as were devoted to ruin and destruction, for whom there was no redemption — those devoted by God himself, as the idolaters, the seven nations of Canaan, and especially the Amalekites, who were to be put to death.

debates.question: Who are the 'devoted of men' that cannot be redeemed?

sides.position: Persons under the ban of destruction by God's sentence (the Canaanites, Amalek) — not human sacrifice nor those devoted to God's service

themes: None devoted... of men, shall be redeemed
but shall surely be put to death
the ban of God's justice on the incorrigibly wicked

schools.summary: Gill: there were various tithes — the first to the Levites, the second eaten at Jerusalem, and the poor's tithe in the third and sixth years; the tenth of the land's increase, seed and fruit, is the LORD's, holy unto him.

themes: all the tithe of the land... is the LORD's
it is holy unto the LORD
the first portion God's by right (Mal. 3:10)

schools.summary: Gill: this is of redeeming his own tithes, not his neighbour's, with the fifth part added.
Keil & Delitzsch: if a man will redeem ought of his tithes, he shall add the fifth part thereof.

themes: if a man will at all redeem ought of his tithes... add... the fifth part
his own tithe only
the surcharge honouring what was the LORD's

schools.summary: Gill: this concerns the tithe of oxen and sheep, none but clean beasts being tithed.
Clarke: the rabbis picture the flock penned with a narrow gate, the owner standing with a rod dipped in dye, marking every tenth that passed under it as the LORD's.

themes: the tithe of the herd, or of the flock... whatsoever passeth under the rod
the tenth shall be holy unto the LORD
God's portion by a fair, unmanipulated reckoning

schools.summary: Gill: the owner was not to search whether it be good or bad, fat or lean, perfect or blemished, but take it as it was.
Keil & Delitzsch: if he changed it, both it and the change were holy and not to be redeemed; Ellicott: he was not to pick out the good ones for himself.

themes: He shall not search whether it be good or bad, neither shall he change it
both it and the change... shall be holy
the tithe rendered honestly, without human cheating

schools.summary: Gill: the commandments of this chapter, or rather of the whole book, delivered to Moses for the children of Israel, priests and people, given at Mount Sinai after the tabernacle was set up and the LORD spake out of it (Lev. 1:1).

themes: These are the commandments, which the LORD commanded Moses
for the children of Israel in mount Sinai
the holy God instructing the holy nation he redeemed
